source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 14.3.3-14.3.4 verify signature and files (pp. 259-261)

questions.q: Verify the signature of the downloaded package file wget-1.19.5-12.el8_10.x86_64.rpm.
questions.why: rpm -K checks the package signature; a good result prints 'digests signatures OK', a bad one 'DIGESTS SIGNATURES NOT OK'.

questions.q: Why verify a package's signature, and what must be done first?
options: To confirm the package is intact and untampered; you must import the required public key first
To register the package with the vendor
questions.why: Signature verification proves the package wasn't tampered with; the corresponding public key must be imported beforehand (usually by the admin).

questions.q: If a package fails signature verification (NOT OK), what should you do?
options: Do not continue to use/install the package
Edit the package to fix it
questions.why: A failed verification means the package may be tampered with - you should not use it.

questions.q: Check whether the files installed by the chrony package have changed since installation.
questions.why: rpm -V compares installed files against the values recorded in the RPM database; a '.' means that check passed.

questions.q: In rpm -V output, what do the characters 'S', '5' and 'T' indicate when they appear (e.g. 'S.5....T.')?
options: S = file size changed, 5 = MD5 checksum changed, T = modification time (mtime) changed
S = signature, 5 = five files, T = type
S = symlink, 5 = permissions, T = timestamp of access
They are always dots and mean nothing
questions.why: The 9-field column flags changes: S size, M mode/perms, 5 MD5, D device, L link, U owner, G group, T mtime, P capabilities.

questions.q: In rpm -V output, what does a 'c' in the second column mean (e.g. 'S.5....T.  c /etc/chrony.conf')?
options: The file is a configuration file
questions.why: The second column is the file type: c config, d documentation, g ghost, l license, r readme.
